facts: The first World Championship F1 race in North America was the 1958 United States Grand Prix at Sebring.
The Canadian Grand Prix joined the Formula 1 calendar in 1967.
Mexico first hosted a World Championship F1 race in 1963.
Circuit Gilles Villeneuve is named after legendary Canadian driver Gilles Villeneuve.
The Autódromo Hermanos Rodríguez is named after Mexican racing brothers Pedro and Ricardo Rodríguez.
The United States has hosted F1 races at more different circuits than any other country.
COTA in Austin was the first purpose-built F1 circuit in the United States.
COTA’s Turn 1 features a dramatic uphill climb followed by a tight hairpin.
The Canadian GP is known for its long straights and heavy braking zones.
Montreal’s Wall of Champions has claimed multiple world champions.
The Wall of Champions sits at the exit of the final chicane in Montreal.
Mexico’s stadium section runs through a former baseball stadium.
The Autódromo Hermanos Rodríguez sits at over 2,200 meters above sea level.
High altitude in Mexico City reduces air density and affects engine cooling.
Watkins Glen hosted the US Grand Prix from 1961 to 1980.
The US Grand Prix at Indianapolis used part of the famous oval.
The 2005 US Grand Prix saw only six cars start due to tire issues.
Mario Andretti is the only American-born F1 World Champion.
Mario Andretti won his 1978 title with Lotus.
Gilles Villeneuve is considered a Canadian racing icon despite never winning a title.
Jacques Villeneuve won the 1997 F1 World Championship.
Jacques Villeneuve is the only Canadian F1 World Champion.
The Canadian GP is known for unpredictable weather and safety cars.
The 2011 Canadian GP is the longest race in F1 history.
The 2011 race featured six safety cars and a red flag.
The Mexican GP often draws over 300,000 fans on race weekends.
The Mexican GP podium is located inside the stadium section.
COTA’s layout includes corners inspired by Silverstone and Hockenheim.
The US GP at COTA often features major concerts during the weekend.
Lewis Hamilton clinched his third title at COTA in 2015.
Montreal’s circuit is located on an artificial island in the St. Lawrence River.
Île Notre-Dame was built for Expo 67.
Sergio Pérez earned the first Mexican home-race podium in 2021.
Sergio Pérez is the most successful Mexican F1 driver.
Pedro Rodríguez won two F1 Grands Prix for Mexico.
The original Peraltada corner in Mexico was a high-speed, banked challenge.
Modern safety standards led to Peraltada being partially bypassed.
Las Vegas once hosted an F1 race in a hotel parking lot.
North American races attract fans from across the continent.
Groundhogs occasionally wander onto the track at the Canadian GP.
Weather in Montreal can shift rapidly during race weekend.
COTA’s back straight is one of the longest in F1.
Mexico’s main straight allows some of the highest top speeds of the season.
Thin air in Mexico forces teams to run high-downforce wings.
Watkins Glen was known for its fast, flowing layout.
The Canadian GP often plays a key role in mid-season championship battles.
Montreal’s circuit is semi-permanent and uses public roads.
COTA also hosts MotoGP and other major racing series.
The Canadian GP was dropped in 2009 but returned in 2010.
The Mexican GP returned in 2015 after a long absence.
Drivers often use special helmet designs for North American races.
Sergio Pérez receives some of the loudest fan support in F1.
Canadian fans are known for cheering underdog performances.
The US GP is central to F1’s expansion in the American market.
Austin’s “Keep Austin Weird” culture shows up in fan costumes.
Robert Kubica earned his first win at the 2008 Canadian GP.
BMW Sauber’s only F1 win came at the 2008 Canadian GP.
The Wall of Champions earned its name in the late 1990s.
The Mexican GP podium often features traditional music and confetti.
COTA’s Turn 1 is one of the most iconic modern F1 corners.
Fuel consumption used to be a major concern at the Canadian GP.
American F1 drivers include Phil Hill, Dan Gurney, and Alexander Rossi.
Phil Hill was the first American-born F1 World Champion.
Dan Gurney started the tradition of spraying champagne on the podium.
North American races attract crossover fans from IndyCar and NASCAR.
Montreal hosts festivals and street parties during GP weekend.
Mexican GP fans often coordinate massive flag displays.
COTA has been resurfaced multiple times due to soil movement.
Wildlife occasionally disrupts sessions at the Canadian GP.
Phoenix hosted a temporary street circuit for F1 in the early 1990s.
Phoenix races were held in extreme desert heat.
Montreal’s hairpin is one of the best overtaking spots in F1.
The hairpin leads onto a long DRS straight.
Mexico’s stadium section is one of the slowest on the calendar.
The US once hosted three F1 races in a single season.
The Caesars Palace GP was run in a hotel parking lot.
The Canadian GP has produced several dramatic late-race battles.
COTA’s esses section mimics Silverstone’s Maggots and Becketts.
Mexico’s altitude reduces drag, increasing top speeds.
The US GP at Watkins Glen was known for its festive atmosphere.
Montreal’s circuit is surrounded by water on all sides.
The Mexican GP often features mariachi performances.
COTA’s observation tower offers panoramic views of the track.
The Canadian GP is one of the most attended races in North America.
The US GP at Indianapolis once used the oval’s banking in reverse.
Mexico’s Peraltada corner was inspired by Monza’s Parabolica.
COTA’s design was influenced by Hermann Tilke and American engineers.
The Canadian GP has one of the shortest pit lanes in F1.
Mexico’s thin air affects brake cooling significantly.
The US GP at Phoenix featured a young Michael Schumacher in 1991.
Montreal’s circuit is popular for cycling when not used for racing.
The Mexican GP often wins awards for best fan atmosphere.
COTA’s amphitheater hosts concerts year-round.
The Canadian GP has produced multiple first-time pole sitters.
The US GP has been held in nine different American cities.
Mexico City’s altitude forces teams to rethink cooling strategies.
Montreal’s final chicane is one of the most famous in F1.
COTA’s steep hill at Turn 1 rises 133 feet from the start line.
